content.text: Poverty is a multidimensional problem that affects millions of people in India. It goes beyond just lack of income to include hunger, lack of shelter, inability to access healthcare and education, lack of clean water and sanitation, and a sense of helplessness.

content.title: Two Typical Cases of Poverty
content.text: Ram Saran (Urban Case): A daily-wage labourer near Ranchi earning around ₹3,500 per month when employed, supporting a family of six plus elderly parents. His family lives in a one-room rented shack, manages two meals of dal and rice daily, and has only two pairs of clothes each. His elder son works as a helper earning ₹700, while his 10-year-old daughter attends school and cares for younger siblings.

content.title: Lakha Singh (Rural Case)
content.text: A landless labourer near Meerut doing odd jobs for big farmers, earning ₹200 per day or payment in kind. His family of six manages two square meals daily, lives in a kuchha hut, lacks access to healthcare (his father died of TB, mother now suffers from the same disease). Although the village has a primary school, Lakha never attended, but his children now go to school.
Mahatma Gandhi said that India would be truly independent only when the poorest of its people become free of human suffering.

notes.section_title: Poverty as Seen by Social Scientists

content.text: Since poverty has many facets, social scientists look at it through a variety of indicators. Usually, indicators relate to levels of income and consumption. In recent times, other indicators have also been used to recognize whether a person is poor.

content.title: National Multidimensional Poverty Index (NMPI)
content.text: NITI Aayog calculates the National Multidimensional Poverty Index based on three broad indicators with 12 dimensions. A person is considered multidimensionally poor if deprived in any of these indicators.

content.title: Three Broad Indicators of NMPI
items: Health Indicators: Nutrition, child and adolescent mortality, maternal health
Education Indicators: Years of schooling, school attendance
Standard of Living Indicators: Cooking fuel, sanitation, drinking water, housing, electricity, assets, bank account

content.title: Social Exclusion
content.text: Poverty must be seen in terms of the poor having to live only in poor surroundings with other poor people, excluded from enjoying social equality of better-off people. Social exclusion can be both a cause and consequence of poverty. A typical example is the caste system in India where people belonging to certain castes are excluded from equal opportunities.

content.title: Vulnerability
content.text: Vulnerability to poverty describes the greater probability of certain communities or individuals becoming or remaining poor. It is determined by options available for finding alternative living in terms of assets, education, health, and job opportunities. Vulnerable groups face greater risks during natural disasters and economic downturns, and have less ability to handle these risks.

The poverty line is a common method used to measure poverty based on income or consumption levels. A person is considered poor if their income or consumption level falls below a given 'minimum level' necessary to fulfill basic needs. The poverty line varies with time and place, as each country uses an imaginary line appropriate for its existing level of development and accepted minimum social norms.

While determining the poverty line in India, a minimum level of food requirement, clothing, footwear, fuel and electricity, and educational and medical needs were taken into consideration. The formula for food requirement was based on desired calorie requirement.

content.title: Calorie Requirements in India
content.text: The accepted average calorie requirement in India is 2400 calories per person per day in rural areas and 2100 calories per person per day in urban areas. Rural calorie requirements are higher because people engage in more physical work.

Based on global developments, the Government of India now estimates the proportion of poor using 12 developmental indicators through the Multidimensional Poverty Index, which complements income or consumption expenditure-based poverty measurement by measuring and comparing deprivations directly.

content.title: Trends in Multidimensional Poverty
content.text: The ratio of multidimensional poverty has fallen from approximately 55% in 2005-06 to 25% in 2015-16 and to 15% in 2019-21. It is expected to soon reach single digits.

As per official record, 13.5 crore people have escaped multidimensional poverty during 2015-2021. Specifically, deprivation against various indicators has declined more sharply in rural areas than in urban areas.

The proportion of poor people varies significantly across states. States like Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Delhi, Punjab, Himachal Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Haryana, and Maharashtra had a Head Count Ratio (HCR) less than 10% in 2019-21. Remarkable strides have been made by states like Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and Rajasthan.

Different states have followed different approaches to reduce poverty: Kerala focused on human resource development; West Bengal implemented land reform measures; Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu strengthened public distribution of food grains.

The proportion of people below poverty line varies across social groups and economic categories. The most vulnerable social groups are Scheduled Caste (SC) and Scheduled Tribe (ST) households. Among economic groups, rural agricultural labour households and urban casual labour households are the most vulnerable.

content.title: Poverty Among Vulnerable Groups (2011-12)
content.text: 43% of Scheduled Tribes, 34% of casual workers in urban areas, 34% of casual farm labour in rural areas, and 29% of Scheduled Castes are below poverty line. The double disadvantage of being a landless casual wage labour household from SC or ST background highlights the seriousness of the problem.
Apart from these social groups, there is also inequality of incomes within a family. In poor families, women, elderly people, and female infants are often denied equal access to available resources.

International organizations like the World Bank use a uniform standard for the poverty line: minimum availability of the equivalent of $2.15 per person per day. The proportion of people living in extreme economic poverty globally has fallen from 16.27% in 2010 to 9.05% in 2019.

Poverty has declined substantially in China and Southeast Asian countries due to rapid economic growth and massive investments in human resource development. China's poverty ratio reached 0.1% in 2020. In South Asia, poverty declined from 13% in 2017 to 11% in 2021, with the number of poor falling from 233 million to 207 million.

content.title: Head Count Ratio Comparison Among Selected Countries
headers: % of Population below $2.15 a day (2017 PPP)

content.text: In Sub-Saharan Africa, poverty declined from 36.6% in 2017 to 35% in 2019. In Latin America and the Caribbean, poverty increased from 4.4% in 2017 to 4.6% in 2021. Poverty has resurfaced in some former socialist countries like Russia, where it was officially non-existent earlier.

The United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) propose ending poverty of all types by 2030. SDG 1 (No Poverty) includes the target of reducing the proportion of men, women, and children living in poverty in all its dimensions by at least half by 2030. The decline in poverty reflects India's commitment to achieving this goal.

One historical reason for widespread poverty in India is the low level of economic development under British colonial administration. Colonial policies ruined traditional handicrafts and discouraged industrial development, particularly in textiles. The low rate of growth persisted until the 1980s, resulting in fewer job opportunities and low growth rate of incomes, accompanied by high population growth.

With the spread of irrigation and the Green Revolution, many job opportunities were created in agriculture, but the impact was limited to some parts of India. Industries in both public and private sectors provided some jobs, but not enough to absorb all job seekers. Unable to find better jobs in cities, many people started working as rickshaw pullers, vendors, construction workers, and domestic servants with irregular small incomes, leading to growth of slums.

Another feature of high poverty rates has been huge income inequalities, largely due to unequal distribution of land and other resources. Major policy initiatives like land reforms aimed at redistribution of assets in rural areas have not been implemented effectively by most state governments.

Socio-cultural and economic factors also contribute to poverty. People, including the very poor, spend heavily on social obligations and religious ceremonies. Small farmers need money for agricultural inputs and often borrow at high risk, becoming victims of indebtedness. High level of indebtedness is both a cause and effect of poverty.

Removal of poverty has been a major objective of Indian developmental strategy. The current anti-poverty strategy is based on two factors: (i) promotion of economic growth, and (ii) targeted anti-poverty programmes.

Since the 1980s, India's economic growth has been among the fastest in the world. The growth rate jumped from about 3.5% per year in the 1970s to about 6% during the 1980s and 1990s, with rapid growth continuing in the 21st century. Higher growth rates have significantly helped reduce poverty, creating a strong link between economic growth and poverty reduction.
However, growth in the agriculture sector has been much below expectations, which has a direct bearing on poverty since a large number of poor people live in villages and depend on agriculture.

content.title: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), 2005
content.text: Aims to provide 100 days of wage employment to every household to ensure livelihood security in rural areas. It also aims at sustainable development to address drought, deforestation, and soil erosion. One-third of proposed jobs are reserved for women. The wage rate for unskilled manual workers is revised regularly.

content.title: Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Abhiyan (PM Poshan)
content.text: A flagship programme with twin purposes: (i) to improve nutritional requirements, especially calorie and protein intake of children, and (ii) to improve school enrolment and eliminate dropouts. It targets students from Classes I to VIII in government and government-aided schools and special training centres under the Right to Education Act. The scheme encourages poor children from disadvantaged sections to attend school regularly.

content.title: Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA)
content.text: Launched in 2016 to reduce maternal and infant mortality by providing quality antenatal care to pregnant women nationwide. The scheme works towards safe pregnancies and deliveries, addressing gaps in maternal care services. Services are provided on the ninth day of every month, engaging private practitioners as volunteers.

content.title: Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana (PMUY)
content.text: Launched in 2016 to provide clean cooking fuel (LPG) to economically poor households, especially in rural areas, who cook using traditional methods (coal, wood, cow dung). Target beneficiaries are BPL households, SC, ST, forest dwellers, and marginalized groups. The scheme provides a completely free connection including first refill and stove. Connections are issued in the name of the female member, promoting women empowerment, reducing respiratory disorders, and promoting environmental sustainability.

Poverty has declined in India, but poverty reduction remains India's most compelling challenge. Wide disparities exist between rural and urban areas and among states. Certain social and economic groups remain more vulnerable to poverty.

The income-based definition of poverty captures only a limited part of what poverty really means. It is about a 'minimum' subsistence level rather than a 'reasonable' level of living. Many scholars advocate broadening the concept to human poverty, which considers whether people have education, shelter, healthcare, job security, self-confidence, and freedom from caste and gender discrimination.

Worldwide experience shows that with development, the definition of poverty also changes. The National Multidimensional Poverty Index reveals that the number and proportion of multidimensionally poor has declined in India due to various government interventions.

glossary.definition: The minimum level of income or consumption necessary to fulfill basic needs. It varies with time and place based on a country's level of development and accepted minimum social norms.
The proportion of people below the poverty line in a given population, used to estimate the number and percentage of poor people.
A measure of poverty that goes beyond income to include multiple deprivations in health, education, and standard of living. In India, NITI Aayog uses 12 indicators across three broad dimensions.
A process through which individuals or groups are excluded from facilities, benefits, and opportunities that others enjoy. It can be both a cause and consequence of poverty.
A measure describing the greater probability of certain communities or individuals becoming or remaining poor, determined by their options, assets, and ability to handle risks.
Seventeen global goals adopted by the United Nations in 2015, including SDG 1 (No Poverty), to be achieved by 2030.
The minimum number of calories needed for basic nutrition. In India, it is 2400 calories per person per day in rural areas and 2100 in urban areas.
A situation where more people are employed than actually needed, and removing the extra workers does not affect productivity.
The state of being in debt. High indebtedness is both a cause and effect of poverty, as poor people borrow at high risk and become trapped in debt cycles.
A self-reinforcing cycle where poverty leads to lack of education and health, which in turn leads to low productivity and continued poverty across generations.

summary_points: Poverty is a multidimensional problem involving hunger, lack of shelter, inability to access healthcare and education, lack of clean water and sanitation, and a sense of helplessness.

Social scientists study poverty through various indicators including income, consumption, and the National Multidimensional Poverty Index (NMPI) which uses 12 indicators across health, education, and standard of living.

Social exclusion and vulnerability are important concepts—social exclusion denies equal opportunities to certain groups, while vulnerability describes the greater probability of certain communities becoming or remaining poor.

The poverty line in India was traditionally based on calorie requirements (2400 calories in rural areas, 2100 in urban areas), but now includes the Multidimensional Poverty Index that measures deprivations directly.

Poverty in India has declined significantly—consumption-based poverty fell from 45% in 1993-94 to 22% in 2011-12, while multidimensional poverty fell from 25% in 2015-16 to 15% in 2019-21, with 13.5 crore people escaping poverty.

Inter-state disparities in poverty are significant—states like Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Punjab have HCR below 10%, while Bihar, UP, MP, and Rajasthan have shown remarkable improvement but still have higher poverty rates.
Vulnerable groups include Scheduled Castes (29% poor), Scheduled Tribes (43% poor), rural agricultural labourers (34% poor), and urban casual labourers (34% poor).
Globally, extreme poverty (below $2.15 per day) has declined from 16.27% in 2010 to 9.05% in 2019, with China achieving 0.1% poverty and South Asia reducing poverty from 13% to 11%.

Causes of poverty in India include colonial economic exploitation, low economic growth until the 1980s, unequal distribution of land and resources, ineffective land reforms, high population growth, and indebtedness.

Anti-poverty measures include promoting economic growth (which has created a strong link between growth and poverty reduction) and targeted programmes like MGNREGA (100 days wage employment), PM Poshan (school nutrition), and PM Ujjwala (clean cooking fuel).
The concept of poverty is broadening from income-based definitions to human poverty, which considers education, shelter, healthcare, job security, self-confidence, and freedom from discrimination.
